does: This paper investigates how editorial expertise can be integrated into the design of LLM-based information services through participatory methods. The authors conducted design workshops with a Nordic public knowledge institution to develop an LLM-enabled encyclopedia interface, introducing editorial alignment as a design practice that translates editorial standards and values into concrete alignment objectives for technical implementation. The core contribution frames editorial standards as design artifacts that guide LLM behavior, demonstrating how participatory design workshops can help editors shape AI systems to reflect institutional values rather than defaulting to commercial model behaviors. The case study shows practical methods for involving editors in re-alignment processes and documents how this participation can maintain editorial authority over knowledge dissemination in AI-mediated contexts.
connects: This work builds on established traditions in participatory design and AI alignment research while addressing a gap at their intersection. It extends participatory AI frameworks that seek to include diverse stakeholders in AI system design by focusing specifically on the institutional and epistemic stakes of editorial work. The paper responds to concerns raised in recent scholarship about how LLMs trained on broad internet data may misalign with institutional values and editorial practices, drawing on design-focused approaches to AI alignment that move beyond technical safety concerns. It connects to public knowledge institution research examining how libraries, archives and encyclopedias maintain authority and serve communities, while also engaging with debates about editorial function in digital information environments where algorithmic systems increasingly mediate what knowledge gets disseminated and how.
enables: This framework opens avenues for public institutions to maintain editorial agency and authority as LLMs become infrastructure for knowledge services. The editorial alignment approach could extend beyond encyclopedia interfaces to other knowledge dissemination contexts where institutional standards matter—museums, libraries, news organizations, and educational platforms. It suggests pathways for ongoing participation structures where editorial expertise continuously shapes LLM behavior rather than treating alignment as a one-time technical fix. The work points toward broader possibilities for institutional governance of AI systems, demonstrating how domain-specific standards and practices can inform alignment objectives. Future directions include scaling participatory alignment processes across institutions, developing reusable editorial alignment patterns, and examining how this approach could support democratic oversight of AI-mediated information infrastructure.
